tldr: At the headline layer, Belfast anti-immigrant riots triggered by a knife attack by a Sudanese asylum seeker are framed almost universally as a crime-and-disorder event. A strong secondary current — most visible in opinion titles, wire services, and pan-Arab outlets — treats the violence as evidence of far-right identity politics going mainstream across Europe. Fox News uniquely foregrounds the attacker's nationality in both its headlines; The New Arab alone escalates to 'pogroms' language.

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only bucket to foreground the suspect's nationality in the headline itself — 'Sudanese migrant' appears in both Fox News titles, converting the crime event into an explicit immigration-identity frame that no other outlet replicates at the headline level.

Only outlet to use 'pogroms' as a headline descriptor for the Belfast riots — invoking historical ethnic-cleansing language and anchoring the violence in a colonial/persecution frame absent from every other title in the corpus.

Headline references 'alleged sexual assault' and 'Ballymena' with a URL dated 2025/06/10 — this article covers a distinct June 2025 Northern Ireland riot (Romanian-speaking suspects, different city) that has been clustered alongside the June 2026 Belfast stabbing story, suggesting a historical parallel was algorithmically grouped with the current event.

bottom_line: Headline coverage frames Belfast's anti-immigrant riots first as a crime-and-disorder event (riots, arson, water cannon) and second as a symptom of far-right politics going mainstream — with the sharpest editorial divergence between Fox News (nationality-first framing) and The New Arab ('pogroms'), revealing how the same story is sold to radically different audiences at the homepage level.
